input_path: data\papers\The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin is an incubator system whose plant consists of a styrofoam insulated container, a heat‑bed heating element, a fan to distribute heat, two temperature sensors inside the box, and one temperature sensor outside the box. The plant is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. Its objective is to reach a prescribed temperature inside the box and maintain that temperature regardless of the content, making it a representative cyber‑physical system for temperature regulation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides (i) continuous acquisition of sensor data (temperature readings) from the PT, (ii) calibration algorithms that adjust cyber‑physical models of the incubator based on the latest data, (iii) simulation/prediction of temperature dynamics to forecast future states under current operating conditions, (iv) monitoring services that assess whether the system is operating safely and optimally and trigger alerts when violations are detected, (v) support for control adjustments (e.g., updating the Raspberry Pi control commands) based on model‑based insights, (vi) advanced visualisation of temperature trends and model predictions, (vii) reconfigurability to adapt to changes in the environment or workload, and (viii) contributions to predictability, safety, and reduced maintenance of the Physical Twin.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation leverages a Raspberry Pi as the physical controller, temperature sensors for data acquisition, and a publicly available GitHub repository (https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example‑incubator) that contains the up‑to‑date hardware schematics and software source code. The Digital Twin framework integrates calibration algorithms that adjust model parameters, monitoring algorithms that evaluate safety and optimality, and simulation/optimisation models (e.g., differential‑equation based dynamic models) to support prediction and control.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin is realized as a set of co‑located software components on the Raspberry Pi that hosts the physical incubator. A RabbitMQ server acts as the communication middleware (Communication Server) mediating messages between a low‑level driver that directly controls the heatbed and fan hardware and a high‑level controller that implements the temperature regulation logic. The DT component subscribes to the same RabbitMQ topics, receives sensor readings (two internal and one external temperature values), observes actuator commands, and updates its thermal models using calibration algorithms. It can also generate simulated actions that are sent back through the same message bus. This architecture follows a centralized, component‑based constellation where all twin services (data acquisition, model updating, simulation, and decision support) are integrated via the message broker on a single physical node.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The incubator Digital Twin incorporates two analytical simulation models that predict the temperature inside the incubator: a simpler model with two free parameters and a more detailed model with four free parameters that better captures the thermal inertia of the heatbed. Data are obtained from the physical twin via temperature sensors and are collected over time. A calibration algorithm uses these sensor data to estimate the model parameters, updating the models so they reflect the current operating conditions. The calibrated models are then used by optimisation and monitoring algorithms within the DT.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The physical twin contains a heat‑bed that generates heat and a small fan that circulates air inside the insulated box. Both actuators are driven by a controller running on a Raspberry Pi that implements a bang‑bang control law based on two temperature thresholds (lower limit LL and upper limit UL). Control commands (e.g., turn‑on/turn‑off) are sent to the actuators via messages received from a RabbitMQ server, enabling remote actuation from the digital twin. Safety is enforced by the controller’s threshold logic, which prevents heating beyond the upper limit and cooling below the lower limit.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing layer consists of three DS18S20 temperature sensors, two of which are placed inside the insulated incubator box to monitor internal temperature and one external sensor for ambient reference. A low‑level driver on the Raspberry Pi periodically reads each sensor (sampling period defined by the driver, not explicitly quantified) and publishes the collected temperature values to the RabbitMQ communication server. The sensors provide temperature measurements with the resolution and accuracy specified by the DS18S20 datasheet (±0.5 °C typical). Data are timestamped and transmitted in real time to the DT for calibration, monitoring, and control purposes.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is assessed by comparing the predicted temperature trajectories against measured data from the incubator. Experimental results indicated that the four‑parameter model provides higher fidelity in capturing the heatbed’s thermal inertia than the simpler two‑parameter model. Validation is performed through empirical experiments, and calibration algorithms update the model parameters to minimise prediction error. Uncertainty arises from parameter estimation and sensor noise; the DT uses continuous monitoring to detect deviations and apply corrective updates, ensuring ongoing model validity.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data are collected from the physical twin through its sensors over time; the sensor data (e.g., temperature readings) are fed into the digital twin where calibration algorithms update the cyber‑physical models and are subsequently used by monitors and optimisation algorithms.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: When the digital twin’s monitors detect safety or performance violations, it can trigger human operators or automated algorithms to issue corrective actions to the physical twin; the documents do not specify concrete command formats, validation, or acknowledgement mechanisms.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator


1 It is worth noting that models can be described at different levels of abstraction, and typically there is a
tendency that accurate models also are very slow to analyse for example by means of simulation.

represents “Cyber” and “Physical” respectively.


system, including the hardware and software setup. Then we built two models with
calibration method in section 3. section 4 shows two kinds of experimental results.
Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,
we summarise the report in section 6.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extracted description is highly detailed and perfectly matches the source text. It accurately lists the physical components (styrofoam box, heatbed, fan, internal/external sensors) and the controller (Raspberry Pi). It correctly identifies the system's goal to 'reach a certain temperature within a box and regulate it regardless of the content' and characterizes it as a representative cyber-physical system. Quotes supporting this include: 'The physical components in the incubator form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi' and 'The plant of the Incubator consists of... A styrofoam box... A heatbed... A fan... Two temperature sensors... One temperature sensor'.

The extraction accurately synthesizes the Digital Twin services defined in the Introduction (Section 1) and implemented via the models in Section 3. It correctly identifies calibration, simulation/prediction, monitoring, and visualization as key services. Quotes: 'DT provides the ability to run simulations that reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.' 'DT employs calibration algorithms to update the models... inform monitors that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally.' 'DTs increase the value... by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability... safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.'

The extraction correctly identifies the Raspberry Pi and temperature sensors from Section 2.1 ('Raspberry Pi 4 Model B was selected to implement the controller', 'DS18S20 High-Precision 1-Wire Digital Thermometer... was used'). It correctly references the GitHub repository ('The publicly available repository contains the up-to-date hardware and software specifications'). It also accurately describes the use of calibration algorithms and differential-equation based models ('non-linear least squares solver was used to calibrate', 'dynamics model... described by the differential equations'). The description synthesizes these well, though it frames them within the 'Digital Twin framework' which the text notes is not fully detailed in scope.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

block_2_judge.reasoning: The source documents don't contain specific information about temporal aspects of the Digital Twin including synchronization frequencies between physical and digital components, time scales for different services, latency requirements, and temporal granularity needs. While there are some time-related calculations (e.g., 'fan should take 2 seconds to move all the air within the box' and 'periodically reads the temperature data'), these are not directly related to the Digital Twin's time scale but rather to the physical system's behavior.

The source documents don't contain any information about multiple digital twin instances, centralized vs decentralized architectures, coordination mechanisms between twins, or scope of responsibilities for each instance. The document only describes a single Digital Twin implementation for the incubator system.

The extraction provides a detailed description of the system architecture, which is well-supported by the text. It correctly identifies the 'software setup includes a controller, a low-level driver, and a communication server' (Section 2.2) and that the 'RabbitMQ server [is] running on the raspberry pi itself' (Section 2.2.1). The description of the DT 'listen[ing] to the messages being passed' is supported by Section 2.2.1. The extraction accurately captures the centralized, component-based nature of the constellation. However, the claim that the DT 'can also generate simulated actions that are sent back' is not explicitly detailed in the implementation sketch (which focuses on listening and monitoring), representing a slight inference.

The source documents don't contain specific information about integration with external systems beyond the components of the incubator system, data exchange protocols other than RabbitMQ, interoperability standards, dependencies on external systems, or integration patterns with other Digital Twins or enterprise systems. While the document describes internal communication using RabbitMQ ('We used a Rabbit MQ server, running on the raspberry pi itself'), it doesn't discuss horizontal integration with external systems as defined in the characteristic description.

1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the two analytical simulation models (Model A with two parameters, Model B with four parameters) and notes that Model B better captures the thermal inertia of the heatbed. It accurately states that data comes from temperature sensors on the physical twin and that a calibration algorithm (non-linear least squares solver) estimates parameters to update the models. It also correctly notes the models are used by optimisation and monitoring algorithms. Supported by quotes: 'We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters', 'Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the thermal inertia of the heatbed itself', 'DT employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with the best estimate of the parameters'.

The description correctly identifies the heatbed and fan as the physical actuators and describes their functions (heating and air circulation). It accurately details the control mechanism: a Raspberry Pi implementing a bang-bang controller based on lower (LL) and upper (UL) temperature limits. It also correctly captures the communication architecture, where control commands are sent via RabbitMQ. Quotes supporting this include: 'The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller', 'The controller parameters are: LL – Lower limit for temperature; UL – Upper limit for temperature', and 'The controller on the Raspberry Pi is listening from the RabbitMQ server for commands of controlling the fan and the heatbed'. The mention of safety constraints aligns with the controller logic described.

The extraction correctly specifies the sensor type (DS18S20), quantity (three), placement (two inside, one external), and the accuracy (±0.5 °C) found in the text. It accurately describes the Raspberry Pi reading the sensors via a low-level driver and publishing to RabbitMQ. However, the claim that data is 'timestamped and transmitted in real time' is an inference, as the text states the data is 'uploaded to the communication server' and 'periodically reads', but does not explicitly confirm 'real-time' or 'timestamped'. Despite this, the core details are well-supported. Quotes: 'a DS18S20 High-Precision 1-Wire Digital Thermometer... was used', 'accuracy of 0.5 °C', 'three sensors were deployed', 'Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box. One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.', 'The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data... uploaded to the communication server.'

The extraction accurately assesses model fidelity by comparing the two models, noting the four-parameter model's superiority in capturing thermal inertia. It correctly describes validation through empirical experiments, the use of calibration algorithms to minimize prediction error (residual error), and the role of monitoring in detecting deviations. It is well-supported and specific. Supported by quotes: 'Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the thermal inertia of the heatbed itself', 'When the residual error is small enough, the optimised parameters can be obtained', 'The resulting data is then used to inform monitors that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally'.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator

tendency that accurate models also are very slow to analyse for example by means of simulation.

represents “Cyber” and “Physical” respectively.


system, including the hardware and software setup. Then we built two models with
calibration method in section 3. section 4 shows two kinds of experimental results.
Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,
we summarise the report in section 6.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
